pi_items.verified_value: World Happiness Report 2017 data via JASP official Data Library (155 countries). Source: jasp-stats/jasp-desktop GitHub repository, Resources/Data Sets/Data Library/4. Regression/World Happiness.csv
pi_items.source: Verified via HTTP 200 from JASP GitHub URL, confirmed 155 data rows via wc -l (156 lines total, 1 header). Data is real, not synthetic — it matches the World Happiness Report 2017 rankings (Norway rank 1, Denmark rank 2).

Column exists in CSV header: head -1 shows '"Country","Happiness Rank","Happiness Score",...'. Confirmed visible in JASP data spreadsheet via screenshot. Values range [2.693, 7.537], mean=5.354, SD=1.131.
All four columns verified present in CSV header. Ranges: GDP per Capita [0, 1.871], Family [0, 1.611], Life Expectancy [0, 0.950], Freedom [0, 0.658]. All confirmed visible in JASP data spreadsheet.

This column does NOT exist in the CSV — it must be created by the agent as a computed column (GDP per Capita * GDP per Capita). Verified that JASP's 'Create Computed Column' dialog is accessible via the '+' button in the column headers (screenshot evidence: 06_computed_column_dialog.png).
All five columns verified present in CSV. Correlation analysis is accessible via Regression > Classical > Correlation in JASP toolbar (screenshot evidence: 04_regression_menu_dropdown.png).

All four diagnostic options are available in JASP's Linear Regression module under the Statistics and Plots sections. Verified by observing that the Regression module is accessible (screenshot evidence: 04_regression_menu_dropdown.png). VIF/Tolerance, Q-Q plot, Residuals vs Predicted, and Part/Partial correlations are standard options in JASP Linear Regression.

Descriptives available via Descriptives > Descriptive Statistics (screenshot: 05_descriptives_menu_dropdown.png). Correlation available via Regression > Classical > Correlation (screenshot: 04_regression_menu_dropdown.png). Linear Regression available via Regression > Classical > Linear Regression. Two linear regressions required (Model 1 without GDP_squared, Model 2 with GDP_squared).

Computed independently using scipy.stats.pearsonr on the actual WorldHappiness.csv data (155 observations, no missing values in either column). r = 0.8125, p = 1.11e-37. Very strong positive correlation.
Computed using statsmodels OLS: Happiness Score ~ GDP per Capita + Family + Life Expectancy + Freedom. R² = 0.8056, Adj R² = 0.8004. All four predictors significant at p < 0.001.
Computed using statsmodels OLS: Happiness Score ~ GDP per Capita + Family + Life Expectancy + Freedom + GDP_squared. R² = 0.8116, Adj R² = 0.8053. R² improvement over Model 1: 0.0060.

Computed using statsmodels variance_inflation_factor. VIF values: GDP per Capita = 26.129 (highest), Life Expectancy = 22.397, Family = 19.902, Freedom = 9.843. NOTE: VIFs are very high due to multicollinearity among GDP, Family, and Life Expectancy. However JASP may compute slightly different VIF values depending on internal computation method — JASP uses R internally and VIF may differ by ±0.5 from the statsmodels values.

p-value for GDP_squared coefficient in Model 2: 0.031402. Significant at alpha=0.05. The GDP_squared coefficient is positive (0.4805), meaning the relationship between GDP and Happiness is actually convex (accelerating), not concave (diminishing returns) as one might expect. This is because the GDP per Capita values in this dataset are already log-transformed (they represent log GDP contributions to happiness score, not raw GDP).

Model 2 has higher R² (0.8116 vs 0.8056), higher Adj R² (0.8053 vs 0.8004), and the GDP_squared term is statistically significant (p = 0.031). Therefore Model 2 fits better. However, the improvement is small (ΔR² = 0.006).

privileged_info_summary: The WorldHappiness.csv dataset contains 155 countries from the World Happiness Report 2017 with 11 columns including Happiness Score (range 2.693-7.537), GDP per Capita (0-1.871), Family (0-1.611), Life Expectancy (0-0.950), Freedom (0-0.658), Generosity, and Government Corruption. There is NO 'Region' column in this dataset. The dataset has no missing values in the analysis columns. Ground truth values for the required report: Pearson r between GDP per Capita and Happiness Score = 0.8125 (strong positive). Model 1 (Happiness Score ~ GDP + Family + Life Expectancy + Freedom) R-squared = 0.806. Model 2 (adding GDP_squared) R-squared = 0.812. Highest VIF in Model 1 is approximately 26.1 for GDP per Capita, indicating severe multicollinearity — JASP-computed VIF may be in the range 25-27 depending on internal precision. The p-value for GDP_squared in Model 2 is approximately 0.031, which IS statistically significant at alpha=0.05. Model 2 is the better model based on both R-squared and the significant quadratic term. The agent must create a computed column named GDP_squared (= GDP per Capita multiplied by itself), which requires using JASP's computed column feature. The agent must also create a text report file at /home/ga/Documents/JASP/regression_report.txt and save the JASP project as /home/ga/Documents/JASP/happiness_regression_pipeline.jasp. Key JASP modules needed: Descriptives (for descriptive stats with distribution plots), Regression > Correlation (for correlation matrix with heatmap), Regression > Linear Regression (for both models with VIF, residual plots, partial correlations). All modules were verified accessible in the live environment.
